turns.user: We are working in a Go deployment-controller repository. Keep this project context available for the rest of the conversation. The controller reconciles DeploymentPlan custom resources into Kubernetes Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, and PodDisruptionBudgets. A plan has metadata.name, spec.image, spec.replicas, spec.configVersion, spec.strategy, and status.observedGeneration. Reconciliation is idempotent and uses server-side apply with a field manager named deployment-controller. The API client is controller-runtime. Errors from the API are wrapped with operation, namespace, and object name. Unit tests use a fake client and table-driven cases. Metrics include reconcile duration, apply failures, and observed generation lag. Configuration is read from a ConfigMap named controller-settings and validated before any workload is applied. The validation rules are: image must include a registry host, replicas is between one and two hundred, configVersion is a non-empty DNS label, and strategy is either RollingUpdate or Recreate. RollingUpdate uses maxUnavailable one and maxSurge one. Recreate scales the existing Deployment to zero, waits for observed ready replicas to reach zero, then applies the new Deployment. The reconciler writes status conditions Ready, Progressing, and Degraded. Ready is true only when generation is observed, the Deployment has available replicas equal to desired replicas, and the active configVersion matches the plan. Progressing is true while any apply or rollout step is incomplete. Degraded is true for invalid configuration, an apply error, an image pull error, or a rollout timeout. Each condition has observedGeneration, reason, message, and lastTransitionTime. The controller should never update status when neither the condition set nor observedGeneration changes. The work queue may deliver duplicate requests, delete events, and retries after partial API failures. A finalizer removes dependent ConfigMaps only after the Deployment is deleted. The repository uses Go 1.24, context-aware logging, errors.Is for Kubernetes API errors, and no global mutable test state. Start with a focused routine change: add a helper that validates configVersion and write concise table-driven tests for valid, empty, uppercase, and overlong values.
turns.assistant_context: Add validateConfigVersion(value string) error beside the existing settings validation. It rejects an empty value, uppercase characters, and values longer than 63 characters, then uses the existing DNS-label validator for all other cases. The table-driven test covers one valid value and the three invalid cases without changing reconciliation behavior.

turns.user: Now make rollout recovery safe during a regional API outage. A reconcile can apply the new Deployment, lose its response, and retry after another controller instance takes the lease. Design the durable state transitions and fencing or ownership checks needed so Recreate never scales down a newer DeploymentPlan revision. State the invariants and the failure-oriented tests.
turns.assistant_context: Use status.observedGeneration plus an immutable rollout revision annotation as the durable handoff point. Before scaling down, verify the live Deployment annotation equals the plan generation being reconciled; on every retry re-read before each destructive step. Record a Progressing condition for the revision and make stale workers return without mutation. Tests inject apply-response loss, lease handoff, and a newer generation between scale-down and apply; each must leave the newer revision intact.

turns.user: The ownership design is accepted. Make the smallest Go change that adds the immutable rollout revision annotation to the Deployment apply object, then add one focused fake-client test proving the annotation equals the plan generation. Do not redesign the reconciler.
turns.assistant_context: Add the rollout-revision annotation when building the Deployment object, formatting the plan generation as base-10 text. The focused test reconciles one plan and asserts the stored Deployment annotation matches the plan generation. No status or rollout-control behavior changes in this turn.

turns.user: Before implementing the outage tests, review whether the annotation check is linearizable across a stale worker, a cache lag, and two concurrent reconcile attempts. Compare resourceVersion preconditions, Kubernetes Lease ownership, and server-side apply field ownership. Recommend the smallest design that prevents stale destructive writes and explain the remaining failure window.

We are working in a Rust replicated-settings service. Keep this project context available for the rest of the conversation. The service receives SettingsPatch commands over gRPC and persists an append-only log in PostgreSQL. Each command has tenant_id, request_id, actor_id, expected_version, and a JSON patch. A worker materializes the latest settings document and publishes a versioned invalidation event to NATS. Consumers are at-least-once and can receive delayed or duplicated events. The Rust workspace uses tokio, tonic, sqlx, serde, thiserror, tracing, and proptest. The command handler opens a transaction, verifies request_id idempotency, checks expected_version, appends the event, updates the materialized version, commits, then publishes through an outbox relay. The outbox relay marks an event sent only after NATS acknowledges it. The cache stores tenant settings with version and expiry; it must never replace a newer version with an older event. Errors are SettingsError::Validation, Conflict, Database, Serialization, and Transport. Validation rejects unknown top-level keys, malformed durations, empty webhook URLs, and nested feature flags that are not booleans. The gRPC API maps Conflict to aborted, Validation to invalid_argument, and transient database errors to unavailable. Tests use an ephemeral PostgreSQL database for transaction behavior and proptest for JSON validation. Observability includes command latency, idempotency hits, conflict count, outbox lag, cache invalidation age, and the version carried by each event. A background repair job can rebuild the materialized document from the log, but it must not publish events while rebuilding. Start with a routine change: add a serde-defaulted optional audit_note field to SettingsPatch metadata and write two focused deserialization tests for missing and present values.
turns.assistant_context: Add Option<String> audit_note with serde(default) to the patch metadata. The missing-field test confirms older JSON remains valid, and the present-field test confirms the value is retained. No command, event, or cache behavior changes.

turns.user: Now analyze a failure where the transaction commits version 42, the process crashes before the outbox relay publishes, a repair job rebuilds from the log, and a delayed version 41 invalidation arrives after version 42. Define the safety and liveness invariants, durable state transitions, and consumer rules needed to prevent stale cache state or duplicate business effects.
turns.assistant_context: Treat the log append and outbox row as one transaction. Rebuild reads the log into a separate target and does not publish. Consumers apply invalidations only when event.version is greater than their cached version and deduplicate by tenant_id plus version or event id. Safety requires cache version monotonicity and one visible effect per request_id; liveness requires every committed outbox row eventually publishes or is retried.

turns.user: The invariants are accepted. Make the smallest Rust change that adds a helper deciding whether an incoming invalidation version may replace a cached version, and write table-driven unit tests for older, equal, and newer versions. Keep it independent of NATS and PostgreSQL.
turns.assistant_context: Add should_apply_invalidation(cached_version: i64, incoming_version: i64) -> bool that returns incoming_version > cached_version. Tests cover older false, equal false, and newer true. The helper is pure and does not change relay behavior.

turns.user: Before adding the crash tests, reason through whether request_id idempotency and version monotonicity are enough when two different patches use the same expected_version concurrently. Compare serializable transactions, optimistic update predicates, and per-tenant advisory locks. Recommend one approach and identify the linearization point and retry behavior.
